title: DJ 01.01 Django for Everybody: Building Web Applications with Python (7:33)
description:
Django for Everybody is a course designed for students who have already learned Python to build web applications using Django. The course focuses on object-oriented programming through Django's structure, providing a clear learning path for developing functional websites.

Students host their applications on Python Anywhere from the first week, implementing features such as Ajax, image handling, OAuth-based single sign-on, and Bootstrap for responsive design.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 01.02 Charles Severance on AI in Programming Courses (16:16)
description:
Charles Severance, professor at the University of Michigan School of Information, discusses his approach to using AI in programming education. He emphasizes that AI is now an essential part of software development and students must learn to use it effectively to become better programmers, not avoid it.

Severance's teaching strategy includes breaking course material into small, doable chunks using the zone of proximal development, implementing cumulative exams to discourage AI reliance in early weeks, and auto graders that check for specific course techniques. He explains that AI can effectively solve assignments in the early weeks but becomes unable to handle the more complex problems later in the course, requiring students to develop foundational programming skills.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 03.01 Setting Up Django 5.2 on Python Anywhere (31:58)
description:
This lecture provides a step-by-step guide for setting up Django 5.2 on Python Anywhere. It covers creating a virtual environment, installing Django and dependencies using pip, and configuring the shell environment via a .bashrc file to persist the virtual environment. The process also includes cloning sample code from the DJ4E repository.

The tutorial then configures Python Anywhere's web hosting to run the Django application by specifying the source code directory, working directory, and virtual environment. The WSGI configuration is updated to route traffic to the application, enabling access via a URL without local server commands due to the shared hosting environment.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 03.02 Django Autograder: Checking Student Applications (10:42)
description:
The lecture details the Django course autograder system, a PHP script hosted at dj4e.com that uses Learning Tools Interoperability (LTI) to integrate with learning management systems. This autograder sends HTTP requests to the student's Django application (running on Python Anywhere) to validate HTML responses against specified requirements. It performs up to 50 request-response cycles to ensure the application meets the grading criteria and returns a grade to the learning platform if all checks pass.

The autograder focuses on verifying that the student's code adheres to instructions by checking for specific elements in the HTML output. Students must manually test their applications before submitting to the autograder. The lecture demonstrates a real-time example where a student corrected a Python syntax error in their views.py, resulting in a successful autograder test.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 03.04 Debugging Django Applications on Python Anywhere: Startup Failures and Tracebacks (19:38)
description:
The lecture covers two primary error scenarios in Django applications hosted on Python Anywhere: startup failures where the application fails to initialize, and traceback errors where the application runs but crashes during a request. It provides a method to diagnose startup failures using the `python manage.py check` command in the bash console.

The session demonstrates a startup failure caused by a typo in `polls/views.py` (an incorrect import statement for `HttpResponse`). It explains the importance of the virtual environment for Django version and dependencies, and how to interpret tracebacks to identify and fix code errors.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 04.01 Linux Shell Commands for Django Developers (18:27)
description:
This lecture introduces the Linux command line shell as a fundamental interface for server operations, essential for Django developers. It covers basic shell commands such as ls, pwd, cd, rm, and grep (with recursive search), along with features like command line completion and command history. The session explains why understanding the shell is important, as most production servers run on Linux and use command line interfaces.

The lecture differentiates between several interactive environments: the Linux shell (dollar sign prompt), Python interactive shell (REPL), SQLite shell, and Django shell. Each environment has unique prompts and exit commands; for example, the Django shell is a Python environment preloaded with Django application code. Recognizing the current interface helps developers avoid confusion and effectively manage their development workflow.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 05.01 HTML Parsing and Standard Evolution (5:53)
description:
HTML is a hypertext markup language that defines web page structure. In the browser's request-response cycle, user interactions trigger server requests that return HTML responses. These responses are parsed to construct the Document Object Model (DOM), the in-memory representation of the web page.

HTML evolved from early 1990s browsers to modern standards defined by the W3C. The W3C established rules for HTML, including lowercase tag names, double-quoted attributes, and balanced tags. Precise HTML implementation ensures consistent rendering and predictable browser behavior.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 05.02 Basic HTML Document Structure and Syntax (13:53)
description:
The lecture covers the basic structure of an HTML document, starting with the `html` root element that contains the `head` and `body` sections. The `head` holds non-printing information like the page title, styles, and scripts, while the `body` contains the visible content. It introduces common tags such as `p` for paragraphs, `strong` for bold text, and `a` for hyperlinks.

The lecture explains entity references for special characters (e.g., `<` for `‹`), HTML comments (`‹!--`), and the distinction between relative links (e.g., `href` within the same directory) and absolute links (full URLs). It also describes unordered lists using `ul` and `li` tags, tables with `table`, `tr`, `th`, and `td` elements, and the `pre` tag for preserving whitespace and line breaks.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 05.03 Document Object Model vs HTML Source Code (13:34)
description:
The lecture explains the distinction between HTML source code and the Document Object Model (DOM). The source code is the raw text file retrieved from the server, while the DOM is the structured representation generated by the browser during parsing.

The lecture demonstrates that the browser's parsing process may correct errors in the source code (e.g., adding missing tags like `tbody`). It also covers relative links, special characters (such as ampersand, less than, greater than), and common HTML elements including `html`, `head`, `body`, `a`, `p`, `ul`, `li`, `table`, `tr`, `td`, and `img`.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 06.01 CSS: Styling HTML in Web Applications (12:47)
description:
This lecture covers Cascading Style Sheets (CSS) as the layer that styles HTML content in web applications. CSS is fetched during the request-response cycle and applied to the document object model to define visual presentation. The HTML document is parsed into the document object model, which then receives CSS rules to determine the final appearance of the web page.

The lecture emphasizes CSS's importance for accessibility, noting that pages remain navigable without CSS due to clean HTML structure. This separation of concerns allows back-end developers to generate HTML and front-end designers to apply CSS, enabling efficient web application development.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 06.02 Three Methods for Applying CSS in HTML (15:03)
description:
The lecture details three methods for applying CSS rules to HTML: inline styles via the `style` attribute on tags, internal styles using a `style` tag in the `head`, and external stylesheets linked via the `link` element. It explains the CSS cascade, where rules closer to the target element have higher priority.

The lecture emphasizes that `span` and `div` tags have no default styling, enabling precise control over visual presentation. It also covers CSS selectors, including IDs (`#id`), classes (`.class`), and their combinations for targeted styling without excessive class usage.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 06.03 CSS: Images, Colors, and Fonts (13:39)
description:
This lecture covers CSS techniques for styling images, colors, and fonts. It explains how to use floats to position images to the right, manage floating elements with the `clear` property, and define colors using 16 named colors and hexadecimal RGB values. The speaker also discusses font stacks with fallbacks and the use of relative units like `m` for margins.

The lecture includes practical examples for link styling with the `a` tag, including unvisited, visited, hover, and active states. It also touches on browser-specific CSS extensions, such as rounded corners, and mentions tools like Bootstrap for enhancing web page aesthetics through simplified styling.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 06.04 How Cascading Style Sheets Work (13:25)
description:
This lecture explains the cascading mechanism in CSS, where styles are applied based on specificity and source order. It covers the use of selectors to target HTML elements and the distinction between block and inline elements. The lecture also demonstrates basic CSS properties such as margins, padding, and borders.

The lecture includes practical examples of styling web pages using CSS, including the application of classes and IDs. It shows how the Document Object Model (DOM) interacts with CSS styles and provides examples of styling navigation bars.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 06.05 HTML/CSS Examples: Fonts, Colors, and Layout Techniques (25:49)
description:
The lecture covers HTML and CSS fundamentals including font management with system and web fonts, fallback strategies, color specifications using hexadecimal codes, and web-safe colors. It also demonstrates anchor tag states such as unvisited, visited, hover, and active.

The session details CSS layout techniques such as the box model (content, padding, border, margin), floating elements with the clear property, positioning methods (relative, absolute, fixed), and z-index for handling overlapping elements.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 07.01 SQL: Historical Development and Core Principles (18:57)
description:
The lecture traces the historical development of databases from tape-based storage systems with sequential access in the early computing era to disk drives that enabled faster data retrieval. This evolution led to the creation of early database models in the 1960s and 1970s, including index sequential and network approaches. The U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) addressed vendor lock-in by establishing a standard for database interoperability.

SQL, initially known as the Simple Query Language, provides a standardized interface for database operations without exposing physical storage mechanisms. The lecture emphasizes that defining a schema, which specifies column types and structures, allows database systems to optimize storage and retrieval efficiently.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 07.02 SQLite Basic SQL Commands: Create, Insert, Update, Delete (11:44)
description:
This lecture covers the fundamentals of SQLite and basic SQL commands including CREATE TABLE, INSERT, DELETE, UPDATE, and SELECT. It details how to define tables with specific data types such as integer and varchar, along with constraints like not null and primary key.

SQLite stores both database schema and data in a single file. The lecture explains that core SQL commands are portable across database systems and highlights the implied loop behavior in commands like DELETE without a WHERE clause, which deletes all rows.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 07.03 SQLite Command Line: Basic Table Creation and Queries (8:39)
description:
This lecture demonstrates fundamental SQLite command line operations for creating tables, inserting records, and executing basic queries with examples of SELECT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements using WHERE clauses and ORDER BY.

These operations function on a file-based SQLite database without server dependencies, enabling direct inspection and debugging of database structures.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 11.01 Python Objects Terminology Explained (10:40)
description:
This lecture provides a foundational explanation of Python object-oriented programming terminology for beginning programmers. It clarifies that concepts such as objects, methods, classes, and attributes have been used implicitly in early Python work, including with strings and dictionaries.

The session describes objects as self-contained units of data and code, where classes serve as templates for creating instances. It also covers the boundary between object implementation and usage, enabling encapsulation and separation of concerns in application design.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 11.02 Python Classes, Objects, and the Self Keyword (8:21)
description:
This lecture explains Python's fundamental object-oriented programming concepts. A class is a template that defines the structure and behavior of objects. The example code demonstrates creating a PartyAnimal class with an attribute x (initialized to 0) and a method party that increments x and prints its value.

The lecture details the self keyword, which refers to the instance within class methods. When an object is created, self acts as an alias for the instance variable. The dot operator is used for object lookup, and the dir function reveals an object's attributes and methods, including built-in methods with double underscores.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 11.03 Python Object Lifecycle: Constructors and Destructors (6:09)
description:
The lecture explains Python's object lifecycle, covering the automatic creation and destruction of objects. It details the constructor method (__init__), which initializes object attributes when an instance is created, and the destructor method (__del__), which is called when an object is no longer in use. The constructor is commonly used for setting initial values, while destructors are rarely utilized.

The lecture demonstrates how multiple instances of a class each maintain independent copies of instance variables. By defining a constructor with parameters, such as a name attribute, the example shows that each instance (e.g., s and j) has its own state, including a unique value for the instance variable x. The destruction of an object occurs when the reference variable is reassigned, triggering the destructor.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 11.04 Inheritance in Object-Oriented Programming (7:34)
description:
Inheritance is a mechanism in object-oriented programming where a new class (child) inherits attributes and methods from an existing class (parent). This allows developers to reuse existing code without duplicating functionality.

The lecture demonstrates inheritance with a football fan class that extends the party animal class. The football fan class inherits all attributes and methods of the party animal class and adds a points attribute and a touchdown method that increments the points by seven.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 11.05 Python Variables Are Pointers, Not Drawers in CPython (7:29)
description:
The lecture addresses the common oversimplification in Python variable concepts from the "Python for Everybody" course, explaining that in CPython, variables are pointers to objects rather than named drawers in memory.

The id function reveals memory locations of objects. Reassigning a variable (e.g., x = 2000) changes the pointer to a new integer object, while y = x for lists creates a shared reference. A deep copy using list(x) creates an independent copy of the list.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 08.01 Django Models: ORM and Migrations (19:59)
description:
This lecture reviews the fundamentals of SQL and introduces Django's object-relational mapper (ORM) for database interactions. It explains how to define models in Python using the models.py file, which maps to database tables, and how migrations manage schema evolution across different database backends.

Django's ORM simplifies common database operations such as creating, reading, updating, and deleting records through Python objects. Migrations automatically generate and apply database schema changes, ensuring portability between development (SQLite) and production (PostgreSQL or MySQL) environments without manual SQL.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 08.02 Django Migrations: Creating and Applying Database Schema Changes (7:54)
description:
Django migrations are generated by reading models.py and settings.py to describe database schema changes. They are portable across database backends because they provide a logical description of the schema without specific SQL commands.

The migrate command applies migrations in order to update the database, and Django maintains a table to record which migrations have been applied. In development, resetting migrations can be achieved by deleting the migration files and re-running make migrations; for SQLite, the entire database can be reset by deleting the database file.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 08.03 Django Models: Local Library Project Setup (11:31)
description:
This lecture demonstrates creating Django models for a local library project using the Mozilla Developer Network tutorial. It covers defining model classes in models.py, adding fields and foreign keys, and the migration process.

The tutorial shows running migrations to create database tables, verifying the schema with SQLite, and committing changes to Git. It explains that the object-relational mapper converts model definitions into database tables and handles joins automatically.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 15.01 Django Data Modeling: Models, Normalization, and Integer Keys (5:52)
description:
This lecture focuses on data modeling within Django applications, specifically model.py and admin.py. It discusses the importance of efficient data storage and retrieval, illustrating how poor design could lead to extremely slow operations for large-scale applications. The lecture covers database normalization principles, emphasizing the avoidance of string data replication and the use of integer keys for fast inter-table linking.

Real-world examples include a local library data model, an autograder, and the Sakai learning management system. The lecture explains that database normalization, particularly third normal form, is critical for preventing data duplication and ensuring efficient database operations.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 15.02 Avoiding Data Replication in Spreadsheet Models (8:36)
description:
The lecture explains how spreadsheet data models often face issues with vertical replication, where identical information like book titles, languages, and genres is duplicated across multiple rows. This approach becomes inefficient when managing large datasets, causing increased storage needs, higher scanning overhead, and challenges in maintaining data consistency. The example of a library tracking physical book instances illustrates how repeated entries for the same attributes create operational problems as datasets grow.

The solution involves structuring data into relational tables with explicit relationships. By separating book instances from a central `book` table and using `language` tables, the model eliminates duplication. Each `book` instance links to a single `book` entry, with relationships defined by cardinality—such as zero or more `book` instances per `language` entry. This design ensures data integrity and simplifies updates while reducing storage requirements.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 15.03 Representing Database Links with Primary and Foreign Keys (2:46)
description:
The lecture explains how to model relationships between database tables using primary keys and foreign keys. Primary keys uniquely identify each row in a table and are typically auto-incremented and indexed for efficient data retrieval. These keys serve as the destination endpoints of the relationships.

Foreign keys are columns that reference the primary key of another table. By convention, they follow the naming pattern of table name followed by underscore and the word id. Each table must contain a primary key, and multiple foreign keys can exist per table to establish connections between tables.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 15.04 Django Data Models: Foreign Keys and Migrations (9:56)
description:
This lecture explains Django data models, focusing on foreign key fields for linking tables. Django automatically generates ID fields for each model. The null=True parameter allows foreign keys to be nullable, and the on_delete option specifies behavior when a referenced record is deleted (e.g., cascade or set null).

The lecture details the migration process: makemigrations creates portable migration files based on model changes, and migrate applies these to the database. It demonstrates the resulting SQLite database schema, showing how foreign keys are implemented and the database-specific differences in foreign key constraints.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 15.05 Django Shell: Creating and Saving Model Objects with Foreign Keys (7:45)
description:
The lecture explains using the Django shell to create and save model objects. The shell preloads Django settings and applications, enabling in-memory object creation. For instance, a language object is instantiated with a name attribute and saved to the database, generating a primary key.

The lecture demonstrates foreign key relationships by linking a book to a language object via primary key. It shows creating a book instance connected to the language and saving it. The shell also queries model objects by primary key and traverses relationships to access nested attributes like a book's title through its linked language.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: Django Models - Many-To-One Relationships - Music Database
description:
http://www.dj4e.com - Django for Everybody 

Please visit the web site to access free supporting materials, as well as interactive exercises.

title: DJ 08.03 Django Tutorial 2 on Python Anywhere: File Paths and Database Migrations (29:20)
description:
This lecture provides a step-by-step walkthrough of Django Tutorial 2 from the Django project website, specifically adapted for the Python Anywhere platform. It covers the necessary file paths, including the home directory (~), the django_projects folder, and the mysite application structure. The session details the execution of key commands such as python manage.py migrate for database migrations and python manage.py shell for interactive model testing.

The tutorial emphasizes the process of reloading the Python Anywhere application after each file change using the reload button to ensure updates are reflected. It also demonstrates creating database models, applying migrations, and configuring the Django admin interface to manage application data within the Python Anywhere environment.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 08.04 Resetting Django Database When Migrations Fail (5:47)
description:
This lecture explains how to reset a Django database when migrations or migrate commands fail. The procedure requires deleting the migration files (e.g., numbered Python migration files) and the database file (db.sqlite3).

Before resetting, the models must be verified with python manage.py check. Then, the process runs python manage.py makemigrations to generate new migration files and python manage.py migrate to rebuild the database. This reset method discards all existing data and reinitializes the database from the models.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 09.01 Model-View-Controller Pattern in Web Development (7:07)
description:
The lecture explains the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern as a set of terms used in web development to describe separation of concerns in web server applications. It defines the controller as the component handling request flow and routing decisions, the view as the output (typically HTML) presented to users, and the model as the data layer interacting with databases.

The lecture demonstrates how Django implements the MVC pattern with urls.py acting as the controller, views.py handling both controller logic and view rendering, and models.py managing database operations. MVC is described as a universal concept independent of programming languages and operating systems.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.01 Django Views and Templates: Application Output (6:59)
description:
This lecture covers the implementation of Django views and templates for producing application output. Views, defined in views.py, handle incoming requests and generate HTML responses by rendering templates. The URL dispatcher routes URLs to the appropriate view functions or classes.

The lecture details the Django project structure, where applications are organized within a project. Views interact with the request object to process data, may use models for database operations, and render templates to produce the final HTML output.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.02 Django Template Views and Request Objects (6:19)
description:
The lecture explains Django's template view functionality, which serves static HTML files without Python code. This view uses a template file in the application's templates directory and requires no database interactions. The template path includes the application name to avoid conflicts with other applications.

The lecture demonstrates how Django views receive a request object containing incoming request data. It shows accessing GET query parameters via request.GET and constructing an HTTP response, which can be a string of HTML or a redirect object.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.03 XSS prevention with HTML escaping in Django (15:36)
description:
This lecture describes a cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability that occurs when user input is directly inserted into HTML responses without escaping. Attackers can inject malicious JavaScript that executes in the victim's browser, potentially leading to unauthorized actions.

Django prevents this vulnerability by escaping user input using HTML entities. The lecture also covers Django's URL parameter parsing and 302 HTTP redirects as part of the framework's routing and response handling.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.04 Django Templates: Rendering and Context (10:05)
description:
Django templates provide a mechanism for dynamically generating HTML using a template engine. The process involves passing data in a context (a dictionary) to a template file, which then substitutes variables with double curly braces syntax to produce the final HTML output.

Template names are global across the Django project, so applications prefix template names with their application name to avoid conflicts. The template language also supports conditional logic, as demonstrated in the lecture example.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.05 Django Template Language: Variable Substitution and Automatic Escaping (10:59)
description:
The lecture explains Django's default template language using double curly braces for variable substitution. This system automatically escapes user-generated content to prevent HTML injection, with the safe filter ({{ variable|safe }}) used to avoid escaping when content is trusted.

The template system supports context variables, for loops, conditional logic, and hierarchical object traversal. Template inheritance enables reusing common text across templates through block overrides.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.06 Template Inheritance in Django: Base and Extended Templates (6:05)
description:
Template inheritance in Django allows developers to define reusable HTML structures through base templates and child templates that extend them. This technique reduces code duplication by capturing common elements in a single base template and reusing that structure across multiple pages.

Child templates use block tags to replace specific sections of the base template. For example, navigation menus defined in the base template can be overridden in child templates without repeating HTML code, ensuring consistency while allowing customization of individual page elements.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 10.07 Django URL Mapping and Reversing: Generating Links (12:52)
description:
Django URL mapping and URL reversing generate links between views without hardcoding URL strings. This technique enhances maintainability by enabling applications to be reused across projects without requiring updates to link strings when the application structure changes.

Named URLs in urls.py files are used for reverse lookups via the reverse and reverse_lazy functions. Namespaces resolve conflicts when multiple applications share the same name, and templates generate URLs using the {% url %} tag.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 16.03 Django Login Page Template and Authentication Mixins (10:16)
description:
The lecture explains Django's login page template configuration, noting that the template registration/login.html is globally accessible across applications. It handles form submissions, error messages, and the next parameter for redirecting after login. The template uses the user context variable for authenticated sessions and passes the next value to the login URL.

The lecture details Django's login required mixin for view protection, which automatically redirects unauthenticated users to the login page with the next parameter. This mixin is the preferred method over manual authentication checks, ensuring secure access to views with minimal code.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 03.03 Django Application Structure Overview (7:04)
description:
This lecture explains the standard folder structure and key files in a Django project. Django organizes applications into projects, with each project containing one or more reusable applications. The project directory includes settings.py for configuration, urls.py for routing, and wsgi.py for the web server interface. Applications contain files such as models.py for data models, views.py for request handling, and templates for HTML rendering.

Django processes web requests by routing them to the appropriate application view via urls.py. The view then interacts with models to handle data and renders a template to produce the HTTP response. This model-view-controller pattern organizes the application into distinct components: models for data storage, views for request processing, and templates for HTML output.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.01 Web Request-Response Cycle (6:04)
description:
This lecture introduces the request-response cycle as the fundamental communication pattern in web applications. It details how a browser initiates a request to a server and receives a response to render a web page.

The lecture explains the role of network sockets in the request-response process and how browsers interpret HTML, apply CSS, and execute JavaScript to produce the visual representation of the page.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.02 Network Sockets as Computer Phone Calls (6:34)
description:
Network sockets are a software abstraction that enables temporary communication between computers, modeled after phone calls: establishing a connection, exchanging data, and then disconnecting. This design, developed in the 1960s, avoids the need for permanent connections and scales efficiently across the internet.

Each computer has an IP address (either IPv4 or IPv6) and applications use TCP port numbers to specify services. For instance, port 80 handles HTTP traffic, port 443 handles HTTPS, port 25 is used for email servers, and port 23 is for Telnet, illustrating how diverse protocols operate over the same network infrastructure.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.03 HTTP Protocol: History and URL Structure (16:28)
description:
Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) was invented in 1990 by Tim Berners-Lee and Robert Caillou at CERN. This protocol enabled browsers to communicate with servers using a simple mechanism, introducing the Uniform Resource Locator (URL) structure that specifies the protocol, host (domain name), and document. The URL resolves domain names to IP addresses and supports multiple protocols.

HTTP uses a request-response mechanism where clients send requests (including headers) to servers, which return status lines (e.g., 200 OK or 404 Not Found) and response headers followed by document data. The internet's infrastructure relies on open standards defined by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) through Request for Comments (RFCs), ensuring interoperability without proprietary constraints.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.04 Simple Python HTTP Client with Sockets (8:39)
description:
This lecture details a simple Python HTTP client implementation using socket programming. It shows how to connect to a server (e.g., data.pr4e.org on port 80), send an HTTP GET request with the HTTP/1.0 protocol, and receive the server's response.

The implementation handles UTF-8 encoding for network transmission and decoding for Python's Unicode strings. The lecture notes that this client code serves as a basic debugging tool but emphasizes the use of browser developer tools for inspecting HTTP requests and responses in web development.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.05 Simple Python Web Server Using Sockets (16:07)
description:
The lecture implements a minimal Python web server using socket programming that responds to HTTP GET requests with a static "hello world" HTML page.

The server listens for incoming connections on a port and sends a 200 OK response with UTF-8 encoded HTML content for each GET request. A client application is built to send HTTP GET requests to this server via socket connections.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.06 Browser Debugger Console: Access in Firefox and Chrome (9:43)
description:
This lecture demonstrates the browser debugger console in Firefox and Chrome. It covers accessing the console via browser menus and its key features including the document object model, JavaScript console, and network request inspection.

The lecture explains the request-response cycle with examples of HTTP GET requests, status codes (200, 404, 500), and HTTP headers. Complex web pages require multiple requests (e.g., 44 requests for 1.6 megabytes of content) that take nearly one second to download, and the distinction between the server's raw response and the browser's parsed document object model.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.08 Mini-Django: A Minimal Django Web Server for HTTP and Routing (36:59)
description:
Mini-Django is a minimal Django web server implementation with fewer than 200 lines of Python. It was designed to help learners understand Django's internal architecture by illustrating the core HTTP request-response cycle, routing mechanisms, and view functions without the complexity of the full framework.

The framework includes a router (urls.py) that maps HTTP request paths to view functions. Each view function processes an HTTP request and returns an HTTP response, and the server handles common HTTP errors such as 404 not found and 500 internal server errors. This minimal implementation provides a clear example of how Django's abstraction layer works.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 12.01 Django Generic Views: One Line Code for Reusable Model Views (16:28)
description:
This lecture explains how Django generic views enable reusable model list and detail views. It demonstrates the use of a single generic view class that specifies a model attribute to handle multiple models, reducing code duplication through the DRY principle.

By inheriting from a generic view class and setting the model attribute, developers achieve one line of code per view. This implementation automatically queries the database and passes context variables to templates, ensuring consistent user interfaces for different models.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 14.01 Cookies and Sessions: Browser and Server Concepts (9:43)
description:
Cookies are a browser-based storage mechanism that allows web servers to track a specific browser session. They are set by the server in response to a request and sent with subsequent requests to maintain state, enabling the server to identify the browser connection without persistent connections.

Cookies can be session cookies, which expire when the browser is closed, or persistent cookies, which have a fixed expiration date. The lecture illustrates how Django handles cookies using request.cookies and sets cookies in response headers, demonstrating the distinction between session and persistent cookies.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 14.02 Django Sessions: Session Cookies and Storage (12:33)
description:
Django sessions enable persistent storage of user-specific data across multiple HTTP request-response cycles. Each session is identified by a session ID, a large random number stored in a browser cookie. The server uses this ID to retrieve the session data from storage.

Django's SessionMiddleware handles session creation, lookup, and management. Sessions are stored in a database as base64-encoded JSON strings. Developers access session data via request.session in views, which functions as a dictionary for key-value storage.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 16.01 Django Superuser Creation and Authentication Basics (3:45)
description:
This lecture covers creating a Django superuser, a user with full permissions. The superuser is created with a username and email address, which serves for password recovery. The superuser is stored in the database and can be removed when the database is reset.

Django uses SQLite for development, where wiping the database removes all user data including superusers. To reset, developers must delete the SQLite database file, run migrations to recreate tables, and recreate the superuser. The admin interface provides built-in user management, and the lecture notes that local users are used for short-term projects before moving to social logins.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 16.02 Django User Login and Logout (7:23)
description:
Django implements user authentication using sessions and the django.contrib.auth app. The lecture clarifies that sessions store user state between requests, while login updates the session with user credentials. Project-level URL configuration in project/urls.py is necessary to register the login and logout views.

The reverse function is used to look up login and logout views by name. The next parameter in login and logout URLs specifies the redirect URL after authentication. Templates automatically inject a user object with attributes such as isAuthenticated, id, and email.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.01 HTML Forms and HTTP Methods in Django (8:19)
description:
Forms in HTML provide user input fields that can be submitted to a server via HTTP methods. The two primary methods are GET, which appends query parameters to the URL (e.g., ?guess=42), and POST, which sends data in the request body. Django handles both by parsing the data into dictionaries accessible via request.get (for GET parameters) and request.post (for POST parameters).

GET requests are idempotent and intended for read operations, making them suitable for bookmarking and web spiders. POST requests, used for creating or modifying data, should not be bookmarked as they change the system state. This distinction prevents unintended data deletions when web spiders follow links.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.02 HTML Form Elements and Validation (11:49)
description:
This lecture covers fundamental HTML form elements including text fields, password fields, radio buttons, checkboxes, dropdown lists, text areas, and submit buttons. It explains how the `name` attribute defines the key for form data submission and the `id` attribute associates labels with fields.

The lecture also discusses HTML5 input types such as color, date, email, number, and URL, along with their client-side validation. These validations run in the browser before form submission, ensuring data integrity by rejecting invalid inputs.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.03 Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) in Web Forms (5:52)
description:
Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) is a security vulnerability where a rogue site tricks a user into submitting form data to a legitimate server without their knowledge. This occurs because the browser automatically sends the user's session cookie with each request, allowing attackers to exploit the victim's authenticated session.

The defense mechanism generates a unique random token stored in the session. Every form submitted by the legitimate server includes this token in a hidden input field. Upon receiving a form submission, the server checks the token against the session store. If the tokens do not match, the request is rejected. Django provides built-in CSRF protection by default when session middleware is active.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.04 Django CSRF Token Implementation for Forms (9:16)
description:
This lecture explains the implementation of Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) protection in Django, focusing on the default behavior for POST requests and two methods for token inclusion. It details that CSRF processing is enabled by default, requiring explicit handling when forms are submitted. The manual method involves adding a hidden input field with the token value, while the recommended approach uses Django's `csrf_token` template tag to automatically generate and include the token.

The lecture demonstrates a CSRF-protected guessing game example. When the form is submitted with the token, the request is validated by the CSRF middleware before processing, ensuring only legitimate POST requests with matching tokens are accepted. This prevents unauthorized form submissions by verifying the token's integrity against the session.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.05 POST-REDIRECT-GET: Preventing Form Resubmission on Page Refresh (3:26)
description:
When a web form is submitted via a POST request and the server returns a 200 status with HTML content, refreshing the browser page triggers a resubmission of the POST request. This can cause unintended duplicate transactions, such as transferring funds multiple times. The browser displays a warning popup to prevent accidental resubmission, but the user may not understand why the refresh operation is problematic.

The solution is the POST-REDIRECT-GET pattern. After processing a POST request, the server redirects the user to a GET request for the same resource. This ensures that refreshing the page does not resubmit the form data, eliminating duplicate submissions and improving user experience.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 13.06 POST Redirect Pattern for Form Submissions (8:04)
description:
This lecture explains how to prevent double form submissions when a user refreshes a page after a POST request. The solution involves redirecting the browser to the same page with a 302 status code instead of returning HTML. This redirect causes the browser to perform a GET request on the same URL, preventing the form data from being submitted twice.

The implementation uses sessions to store a flash message during the POST request, which is then displayed in the subsequent GET request. After the message is shown, it is deleted from the session to avoid re-display. This pattern is called the flash message pattern and is a standard technique for handling form submissions without double submissions.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 17.01 Django Forms: Validation and Template Rendering (13:26)
description:
Django forms are a critical component for handling user input in web applications. They generate HTML, validate data, and integrate with models, views, and templates. The lecture covers the form workflow, including data validation and error handling for create, update, and delete operations.

The lecture demonstrates the form processing cycle: generating form HTML in templates, validating POST data, and responding with redirects or error messages. It also explains the use of CSRF tokens and the form object's role in connecting templates and browsers.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 18.01 Django Autos Assignment: Implementing CRUD with Generic Views (36:13)
description:
This lecture walks through the Django autos sample application, covering the implementation of models, form handling, and URL routing. It demonstrates how generic views can efficiently handle create, read, update, and delete operations.

The lecture emphasizes understanding the underlying Django mechanisms to avoid copy-pasting repetitive code. By using generic views and LoginRequiredMixin, developers can implement CRUD functionality with minimal configuration.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 17.02 Django Server-Side Form Validation (9:13)
description:
This lecture covers server-side form validation in Django. When a form is submitted via POST, the server validates the data against defined rules. If validation fails, the server returns the form with error messages for correction.

The lecture explains how Django form objects process POST data and handle validation errors. Successful submissions result in a redirect to a success page. It also notes that Django returns a 200 status code after POST when no data changes, which can cause double processing if the page is refreshed.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.07 Django Application Structure and Request-Response Cycle (17:15)
description:
This lecture covers the fundamental flow of a Django application, describing the request-response cycle and project structure. It explains how browser requests are processed by a Django application running on a server, which routes the request to the appropriate application and returns a response.

The application is organized into a project directory containing settings.py for configuration and multiple applications. Each application has its own urls.py for routing, views.py for handling requests, and templates for rendering responses.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 02.09 Virtual Hosting: How a Single Server Hosts Multiple Domains (4:31)
description:
Virtual hosting enables a single physical server to host multiple domain names by using the host header in HTTP requests. This technique allows organizations like Python Anywhere to support a large number of users on limited hardware resources.

The server software (e.g., nginx) routes requests based on the host header to the appropriate application. In Python Anywhere, the system-managed wsgi configuration file specifies the application's home directory, ensuring each application runs in isolation within the shared server environment.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 20.01 Django Marketplace Initial Project Setup with Virtual Environment (9:45)
description:
This lecture details the initial configuration steps for the Django Marketplace project. It covers creating a new Django project named Market with a configuration directory structure, setting up a Python 3.13 virtual environment, and installing Django 5.2. Key steps include generating and applying migrations, creating a superuser with the username admin, and configuring WSGI to switch from the MySite project to Market.

After completing these steps, the Marketplace application runs locally and is accessible via the web server. The setup includes an admin interface for user management and provides the foundation for developing the Marketplace features as specified in the assignment.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 23.02 Django model ownership with user-specific rows (30:13)
description:
This lecture demonstrates Django's implementation of user-specific model rows. The Article model contains a foreign key field linking to the Django auth.User model, ensuring each instance belongs to a single user. The system uses a reusable ownership pattern that extends generic views to enforce user-specific access.

The owner create view automatically sets the user field to the current authenticated user during form validation. Update and delete views apply a queryset filter to verify ownership before processing requests. This pattern is designed to be used across multiple models without modifying the owner.py file.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 22.01 Django Owned Rows Implementation Using Inheritance (6:02)
description:
Django owned rows are database rows that users can view but can only edit or delete if they are the owner. This pattern is essential for applications requiring data ownership, such as classified ad systems.

The implementation uses inheritance to extend Django's generic list view, adding an owner field to conditionally display edit and delete buttons for the current user. This approach avoids code repetition by creating a specialized view class (OwnerListView), though additional security measures (like permission checks) are necessary to prevent unauthorized edits.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 22.02 How to Extend Django Generic Edit Views with Method Overrides (10:17)
description:
The lecture explains the internal method flow of Django generic edit views and demonstrates two techniques for extending their behavior: complete method replacement and context augmentation.

Complete method replacement involves overriding a method entirely to change its functionality, such as altering the data source by returning horse objects instead of car objects. Context augmentation allows adding new context variables to the existing context without replacing the parent method's output, by first calling the parent method and then extending the return value.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 22.03 Django Owner Views Implementation with User Authentication (13:03)
description:
The lecture details implementing Django views that enforce article ownership by adding an owner foreign key to the model. The model includes an owner field with cascade delete behavior, ensuring articles are automatically removed when the associated user is deleted. The views extend generic Django views to handle ownership constraints while maintaining database integrity.

Security is enforced through queryset filtering in update and delete views to restrict access to the current user's articles. During article creation, the form saves the owner field as the authenticated user's primary key before committing to the database. This pattern leverages Django's inheritance to minimize boilerplate while ensuring only authorized users can modify their owned content.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 23.03 Django Bootstrap Menu Implementation with Template Inheritance (14:21)
description:
This lecture demonstrates implementing a Bootstrap menu in Django using template inheritance. The menu is defined in a template that extends base_menu.html, which itself inherits from a Bootstrap template. Navigation links are rendered within the navbar block, and the current page is highlighted by applying the active class based on the request path.

The implementation handles user authentication by displaying a Gravatar image for authenticated users. A custom template tag generates a Gravatar URL using an MD5 hash of the user's email address, ensuring the email is not exposed while providing a user-specific avatar image.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 23.01 Crispy Forms: Django Bootstrap 5 Form Rendering (9:14)
description:
Crispy Forms is a Django library that generates styled HTML and CSS for form rendering using Bootstrap 5. It processes a Django form object through a template tag to produce a visually consistent form without requiring manual HTML or CSS code.

Implementation requires installing the Crispy Forms library and Bootstrap 5 template pack via pip, adding crispy_forms to the installed apps in settings.py, and including the load crispy forms template tag in the template. The form object is then rendered using the pipe operator form | crispy to generate the form with required fields and a submit button.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 27.01 Django Data Modeling: Many-to-Many Relationships (5:34)
description:
This lecture covers the implementation of many-to-many relationships in Django. It explains that one-to-many relationships are inadequate for scenarios where an entity can have multiple related items (such as books with multiple authors or genres) because they cannot handle arbitrary numbers of relationships.

In Django, many-to-many relationships are defined using the ManyToManyField in the model.py file. This allows models to represent multiple associations without fixed limits, ensuring the database can accommodate varying numbers of related objects.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 27.02 Django Many-to-Many Relationships with Junction Tables (12:35)
description:
Many-to-many relationships cannot be directly modeled in relational databases. The solution involves breaking the relationship into two one-to-many relationships and using a junction table to store connections between entities.

In Django, a many-to-many field creates a junction table and virtual attributes for related objects. The junction table supports on delete cascade for automatic cleanup during deletions, and the data model can specify constraints like one dot dot star (minimum one) or zero dot dot star (minimum zero) for relationships.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 27.03 Django Many-to-Many Relationship with Role and Timestamps (8:38)
description:
The lecture demonstrates a many-to-many relationship between persons and courses in an educational autograder application. This relationship is implemented using a through table (membership table) that includes foreign keys to persons and courses, along with created_at and updated_at timestamp fields. The role field in the membership table uses integer values to represent predefined roles (learner, teaching assistant, instructor, admin) without string replication.

The example shows how to interact with this model in Django's shell, including creating persons and courses, adding memberships with specific roles, and querying membership data. The integer role system employs a tuple of choices to map integers to role names in the admin interface, ensuring consistent representation and avoiding code duplication.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

http://www.dj4e.com - Django for Everybody - Using Python and Django to build web sites 

DJ 04 Many 04 Batch

title: DJ 24.01 JavaScript: History, Syntax, and Browser Applications (9:59)
description:
JavaScript was created in 1995 by Brendan Eich in 10 days as a programming language for web browsers. It was designed to run within the browser alongside HTML and CSS and uses a C-like syntax with semicolons and curly braces.

This language interacts with the browser's DOM for client-side operations and can be extended to server-side applications using Node.js.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: Computing Conversations with Brendan Eich
description:
Computer's multimedia editor Charles Severance speaks with Mozilla CTO Brendan Eich about how the latter created JavaScript in 1995. As HTML5 emerges, it's entirely possible that JavaScript will soon become a dominant programming language for both mobile and desktop applications, which is impressive for a language Eich developed in 10 days nearly 20 years ago. From Computer's February 2012 issue: http://opac.ieeecomputersociety.org/opac?year=2012&volume=45&issue=2&acronym=computer

Visit Computer: www.computer.org/computer

title: DJ 24.02 JavaScript DOM and Event Handling in Web Browsers (14:02)
description:
This lecture covers the fundamentals of JavaScript in web browsers, including how it interacts with HTML and the Document Object Model (DOM). It demonstrates the use of `document.write` to output text to the screen, the `alert` function for debugging, and the three primary methods for embedding JavaScript: inline within `html` (using a `script` tag), as event handlers (e.g., `onClick`), or in external `.js` files.

The lecture explains browser debugging techniques, such as using the console for error messages and `console.log` for non-blocking debug output. It also addresses how JavaScript syntax errors halt execution without user notification and the critical role of the browser console in identifying and resolving errors during development.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 24.03 JavaScript Syntax: Comments, Variables, and Numbers (16:57)
description:
JavaScript syntax includes two comment styles: multi-line comments using /* ... */ and single-line comments using //. Variable names are case-sensitive and can consist of letters, numbers, underscores, and dollar signs (but should not start with a number). JavaScript ignores whitespace and new lines during parsing, and statements typically end with semicolons as a convention from C-style languages.

JavaScript has a single numeric type (number) that handles floating-point arithmetic. Type coercion occurs automatically in operations, such as adding a number and a string, which may produce NaN (Not a Number) for invalid numeric operations. The typeof operator and strict equality (===) are used for type checking and comparisons.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 24.04 JavaScript Functions and Arrays: Scoping (7:02)
description:
This lecture covers JavaScript functions and arrays. Functions are defined using the function keyword followed by a name, parameters, and a body enclosed in curly braces. Functions can return values via the return statement. A key difference from many other languages is that variables defined inside a function without the var keyword are global by default, which is then explicitly scoped to local using the var keyword.

JavaScript arrays are linear structures that resemble Python lists. Arrays are not associative; objects are used for key-value pairs. The push method appends elements to the end of an array.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 24.05 JavaScript Control Structures: If Statements, Loops, and Logical Operators (6:37)
description:
The lecture explains JavaScript control structures, including if statements and logical operators. It describes the use of equality (==), strict equality (===), inequality (!=), logical AND (&&), and logical OR (||) operators, and how blocks (curly braces) define code executed when a condition is true. The lecture also covers multi-way if statements with else if clauses and optional else blocks.

The lecture details while loops (zero-trip loops) and for loops (counted loops), emphasizing the need to manage iteration variables to avoid infinite loops. It explains the break and continue statements, and how to iterate over JavaScript objects (definite loops) versus arrays (counted loops).

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 25.01 Object-Oriented Programming Concepts in JavaScript (4:20)
description:
This lecture explains core object-oriented programming concepts in JavaScript, including classes as blueprints for creating objects, instances as concrete manifestations of these classes, and methods as code that operate on object data. It also covers inheritance through subclasses.

JavaScript supports first-class functions, allowing functions to be treated as data that can be assigned to variables and passed as arguments. This design aligns with Lisp's historical approach to unifying code and data, where functions and other values are treated uniformly.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 25.02 JavaScript Classes: Function-Based Implementation and Instances (13:13)
description:
JavaScript classes are implemented using the function keyword, forming a function-based template for object creation. The class body defines instance variables and methods, with the this keyword referring to the instance object being constructed.

The constructor function runs during instance creation to initialize attributes. Each instance maintains independent attribute values, ensuring that multiple instances operate with distinct data states when methods are called.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 26.01 Django CRUD Image Upload Implementation (33:06)
description:
This lecture demonstrates implementing image uploads in a Django CRUD application. The model includes a binary field for storing image data and a content type field (string) for the MIME type. The admin interface is customized to hide fields that would cause issues with image handling, and form validation uses client-side JavaScript to check file size (under 2 MB).

The application handles file uploads via Django's form processing, with server-side validation to ensure file size and content type. The detail view streams images using stored content type and content length headers, and a simple overlay modal is implemented for larger image viewing with minimal CSS and JavaScript.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 28.01 Django Many-to-Many Relationships with Junction Tables (25:28)
description:
This lecture walks through a Django implementation of many-to-many relationships using a junction table. The example application features a forums system where comments are stored in a separate table that connects users and forums via two foreign keys.

The code demonstrates the use of related_name to create an attribute on the user model for accessing comments and covers the view and URL patterns for comment creation and deletion, including a post-redirect pattern for deletion.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.01 JavaScript Execution Model: Events, DOM, and First-Class Functions (8:24)
description:
JavaScript in browsers operates with a cooperative multitasking model, where code execution is triggered by events rather than waiting for operations to complete. This contrasts with traditional programming environments where the operating system manages blocking and non-blocking operations.

The Document Object Model (DOM) is the structure that JavaScript manipulates to update the webpage. First-class functions are critical for event handling, as they enable event handlers to be defined and executed without immediate invocation, supporting asynchronous behavior.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.02 JavaScript DOM and Window Object (5:40)
description:
The lecture describes the Document Object Model (DOM) as the structured representation of a web page's content in the browser. JavaScript interacts with this model by reading, manipulating, and updating elements, which are then rendered through the window. Changes to the DOM are immediately visible to the user.

The window object provides properties for the browser window's dimensions and methods for scrolling. When the DOM exceeds the window's visible area, scroll bars appear due to overflow. JavaScript can dynamically adjust the window and DOM to handle such scenarios.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.03 JavaScript DOM Manipulation: Changing Elements and Styles (8:24)
description:
This lecture demonstrates modifying the Document Object Model in JavaScript by changing element content via `innerHTML`. It shows how to select an element using its ID, update its `innerHTML` with a string, and trigger the change with a click event. The browser instantly updates the DOM after the JavaScript execution completes.

The lecture also covers creating new DOM elements using `createElement` and appending them with `appendChild`, and manipulating CSS styles via `style.display`. It includes a practical example of toggling an element's visibility by switching between block and none display values.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.04 Browser Event Registration: addEventListener vs. onClick and DOMContentLoaded (7:54)
description:
The lecture details the browser's event registration system, explaining that `onClick` is a shortcut attribute for attaching click event handlers, while `addEventListener` provides a more explicit and versatile method for registering events with the DOM.

It also covers the `DOMContentLoaded` event, which fires when the initial HTML document is fully loaded and parsed, and demonstrates the window `resize` event for responsive design applications.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.05 JavaScript Network Events with Fetch and Promises (6:26)
description:
This lecture covers network events in JavaScript, explaining how asynchronous operations are handled when communicating with the network. The fetch API initiates requests, returning a promise that resolves when the request completes.

The example demonstrates a promise chain: the initial fetch returns a promise, which is resolved by a then callback. This callback processes the response, retrieves the text via response.text(), and when that promise resolves, the content is set in a DOM element.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 29.06 Creating Custom Elements with LitElement (11:05)
description:
Creating custom HTML elements in the browser using JavaScript with LitElement enables developers to define reusable components that avoid repetitive HTML structure across web pages. The lecture demonstrates a dynamic greeting component that updates its text based on user input without server interaction.

LitElement simplifies custom element implementation by handling rendering and data binding, allowing components to share styles with the parent document and avoid shadow DOM. This approach enables responsive components that automatically update when their data changes, reducing the need for server-side template processing.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 30.01 AJAX and JSON: Server-Client Data Exchange (8:34)
description:
This lecture covers the use of JSON as a data format in the request-response cycle, contrasting it with HTML as the markup format. JSON serves as a wire format for structured data exchange between servers and clients, enabling serialization of data structures from various programming languages.

The lecture demonstrates a Django view that returns a JSON response with content type application/json. This allows client-side JavaScript to process the data and update the document object model.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 30.02 Django JSON Responses and Fetch API Basics (21:53)
description:
The lecture explains that JSON and Python dictionaries share identical syntax structures and demonstrates how Django views can serialize Python dictionaries into JSON responses. It shows the browser's network tab displaying the raw JSON response and the browser's parsing process.

The lecture covers error handling in Fetch API operations, including invalid URLs, 404 responses, and non-JSON responses. It compares two asynchronous patterns: promise syntax and async/await, highlighting their error handling capabilities and typical use cases.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 30.03 Simple Multi-User Chat Application with Ajax and JSON Polling (8:05)
description:
This lecture demonstrates building a simple multi-user chat application using Django and JavaScript. The application implements polling every four seconds to fetch JSON data from a server endpoint, displaying recent messages in a chat interface. Messages entered in one tab appear asynchronously in another tab, with the server returning an array of message text and time indicators.

The server retrieves the latest 10 messages ordered by creation time and formats time intervals using Django's time library. The client-side JavaScript uses the fetch API to poll the server, clears the chat content div, and appends new messages. The lecture highlights JSON's widespread support across programming languages and its long history in web development.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 30.04 Django Polling Example with AJAX and JSON (16:03)
description:
This lecture demonstrates a Django chat application that uses AJAX polling to fetch and display messages. The example implements a client-side timer using setTimeout to periodically request JSON data from a server, which is then rendered in the browser's DOM.

The implementation uses a polling pattern that sends requests to the server every few seconds. This approach is inefficient for production systems due to high server and network resource usage but effectively illustrates key asynchronous JavaScript concepts and DOM manipulation.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 31.01 Django Favicon Setup and Cache Busting (4:11)
description:
This lecture demonstrates how to set up a custom favicon in Django applications by uploading an icon file to the static directory and configuring the static file server to serve it at static/favicon.ico.

Browser caching can prevent immediate updates to the favicon; adding a cache-busting query parameter like ?x=42 to the favicon URL forces the browser to fetch the new icon.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 31.02 Setting Up GitHub Social Login in Django (8:47)
description:
This lecture details the configuration of GitHub social authentication in Django. It starts with creating a GitHubsettings.py configuration file from the GitHubsettings.dist template and then registering a GitHub OAuth application to obtain client ID and secret credentials.

With the credentials configured in Django settings, the application enables GitHub login. Users are redirected to GitHub for authorization, and upon successful authorization, they are authenticated in the Django application.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 31.03 Django Many-to-Many Favorites with AJAX Implementation (30:20)
description:
This lecture demonstrates a Django implementation for managing user favorites using a many-to-many relationship between `Thing` objects and users. The model includes a unique constraint to ensure each user can favorite a `Thing` only once, preventing duplicate entries. The system uses a `through` model to handle the relationship without additional tables.

The implementation employs AJAX with jQuery to update favorite states without full page refreshes. When a user clicks a favorite button, a POST request is sent to update the database, and the UI dynamically toggles between filled and empty star indicators. Views handle the POST requests with CSRF exemption for simplicity, ensuring only authenticated users can modify favorites.

For more materials, auto graders, and more courses, please see www.masterprogrammer.com.

title: DJ 32.01 Django WELL Sample: Implementing Search with GET Parameters for Idempotent URLs (21:30)
description:
The WELL sample application extends Django's myarts project with a search feature implemented using GET parameters. This approach ensures search URLs are idempotent, meaning they can be safely bookmarked and revisited without altering system state.

The lecture demonstrates how the application filters posts via title and text fields using Django's ORM, and explains a pattern where views are defined in urls.py by extending generic views to override parameters. This eliminates the need for separate view files while improving maintainability.
